name: Flocking
description: A group movement behavior where agents steer using local cohesion, separation, alignment, and goal forces.
design_notes: Flocking sells group life through many small decisions. It needs neighbor queries, squad_id or flock_id grouping, separation radius, steering weights, obstacle avoidance, and deterministic update order so the group does not jitter or explode under load.
design_purpose: Make crowds, wildlife, drones, or swarms move as a believable group.
Create readable mass movement without individually scripting every agent.
Support tactical group behavior such as encircling, scattering, or flowing around obstacles.
player_fantasy: The player feels surrounded by a living group whose motion responds as a collective rather than isolated actors.

parameters.name: flock_id
parameters.description: Group identifier used for neighbor filtering and debug display.

parameters.name: neighbor_query_radius
parameters.description: Radius used to find nearby agents for cohesion, alignment, and separation.

parameters.name: separation_weight
parameters.description: Steering weight that pushes agents away from close neighbors.

parameters.name: alignment_weight
parameters.description: Steering weight that matches heading or velocity with neighbors.

parameters.name: decision_interval_ms
parameters.description: Cadence for updating flock steering independent of render frames.

edge_cases: Agents pile up at narrow passages where separation fights the goal direction.
A flock splits around an obstacle and needs rules for regrouping or accepting multiple subgroups.
Large swarms exceed performance budgets unless neighbor queries use spatial partitioning.
Networked or replayed swarms diverge if agent iteration order is nondeterministic.
common_bugs: Cohesion and separation weights are both high, causing agents to vibrate in place.
Neighbor queries include dead, hidden, or different flock_id agents and distort steering.
Flocking ignores navmesh or collision constraints and pushes agents through walls.
Frame-rate dependent steering makes the same flock behave differently across devices.
balancing_notes: Swarm density affects difficulty as much as individual enemy stats; too much separation may make a swarm harmless.
Readable group motion is usually better than perfect physical realism for combat and navigation.
Lower decision rates can save CPU and also make group movement easier for players to parse.
accessibility_notes: Avoid dense, fast, high-contrast swarms that obscure hazards or overwhelm visual processing without options.
Provide target highlighting or reduced visual clutter when flocking agents are required targets.

implementation_notes.unity: Use spatial hashing, DOTS/jobs, or batched queries for neighbor_lookup; keep flock_id, steering weights, and debug vectors visible in editor gizmos.
implementation_notes.godot: Use grid buckets or physics areas for neighbor queries and update steering on a fixed timer; clamp final velocity before passing it to CharacterBody movement.
implementation_notes.unreal: MassEntity, crowd systems, or custom components can handle large groups; keep steering debug draw and deterministic order for replay-sensitive behavior.
implementation_notes.web: Use typed arrays and spatial grids for neighbor data, update flock steering at decision_interval_ms, and cap agents per frame to protect browser performance.

example_games.title: Pikmin
example_games.note: Uses group movement for many small followers around the player.

example_games.title: StarCraft
example_games.note: Uses grouped unit movement and avoidance as a major readability concern.
